moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, one of the most flexible and subtle first moves in chess. Rather than claiming the center immediately with a pawn move, we develop our knight to an active square that controls key central squares (e5 and d4) while keeping our options open. This move follows the principle of 'move a piece only once in the opening,' and Nf3 is so versatile that it can transpose into countless openings—we're not committing to any particular structure yet. By developing a piece with tempo, we're following the hypermodern approach: we can control the center from afar rather than occupy it immediately.

Black plays the St. George Defense, a rare and somewhat provocative response that immediately prepares b5 to gain queenside space. This move is flexible but slightly passive—it doesn't attack the center or develop a piece, so you should be ready to seize the initiative in the middle. The move signals that Black wants to avoid main-line theory and create an unusual position; historically, it's been championed by players like Moskalenko who love creating practical problems. You'll want to respond by developing purposefully and controlling the center, as Black hasn't yet challenged your spatial advantage.
